notes: Labeled ground truth for both detectors. Each variant root is a self-contained mini-app. 'expect' lists the findings the analyzer should report for that variant (empty = clean). Matching is on (rule, file); line is omitted on purpose to stay robust. See README.md.

cases.summary: A Stripe paymentIntents.create with no idempotency key (double-charges on retry/webhook redelivery); safe passes one in the second RequestOptions argument.

The dominant real pattern: checkout.sessions.create via a Stripe instance imported from a local wrapper (project-level detection), with no idempotency key. The prisma.order.create in the same handler is not a Stripe resource and must not be flagged. Safe passes an idempotency key.

A real secret marked NEXT_PUBLIC_ (NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_SECRET_KEY) is inlined into the client bundle and exposed to the browser. The safe variant has only legitimately-public client vars (Stripe publishable, Supabase anon, a public API URL) plus a server-only secret (no NEXT_PUBLIC_) - none must flag (the precision controls).

A paid LLM API (api.anthropic.com) called directly from a public/ (browser-served) script: the key is exposed to every visitor and there is no server-side rate limit. The safe variant calls the app's own backend (/api/chat) which proxies server-side - no AI host in the client, so it must not flag.
OpenAI call on a public POST route; vulnerable has no limiter, safe wires @upstash/ratelimit in the handler.
Vercel AI SDK (streamText) call; vulnerable has no limiter, safe wires @upstash/ratelimit. The dominant real-world LLM pattern, found by scanning the corpus.

A route exported as a HOF wrapper (export const POST = withX(handler)), the dub/withWorkspace pattern. Safe: the wrapper's own body enforces a limiter, so the route is covered (must be clean). Vulnerable: the wrapper only authenticates, no limiter reachable (must flag). Guards a flagship-app false positive found scanning dub.

An LLM API called by raw fetch (no SDK), the common no-SDK pattern in AI-built apps (found on a real repo). Vulnerable has no limiter; safe rate-limits first; proxy-clean fetches a dynamic env-var URL to a non-LLM backend and must stay clean (the LLM cost is downstream). Validates AI_API_HOSTS detection and its precision boundary.

A newly catalogued provider (groq-sdk, v2): a Groq LLM call on a public POST route. Vulnerable has no limiter; safe enforces @upstash/ratelimit. Proves a v2 catalogue addition flows end-to-end through the detector.
Login with bcrypt.compare; vulnerable has no limiter (auth path raises severity), safe throttles per IP.
The DryRun case: a limiter is configured in the file but never invoked on the route (vulnerable), versus actually invoked (safe). This is the cross-file/wiring case grep cannot resolve.
Edge protection via middleware.ts; vulnerable matcher excludes the route, safe matcher includes it. Tests reachability through the middleware matcher.
The LLM sink lives in a helper (lib/ai.ts), not inline; requires following the handler's call into the helper. Vulnerable has no limiter; safe wires one in the handler.

A sink client built lazily in a helper and used via a local variable: getResend() returns a module-level `new Resend()`, and the route calls client.emails.send() on the result. Requires recognizing a helper that RETURNS a sink client (the lazy-client recall gap). Safe wires @upstash/ratelimit in the handler.
Same as above but the helper is imported via the `@/` path alias; requires reading the project's tsconfig paths to resolve.
Limiter enforced inside a helper reached from the handler (safe, clean) vs a helper that only logs (vulnerable, flag). Tests following limiters across files.
LangChain chat model constructed/invoked in a cross-file helper (mirrors a real corpus repo). Vulnerable has no limiter; safe wires one in the handler.
The common Next.js default matcher (negative lookahead) excludes /api, so the limiter does not cover the LLM route. Must flag (regression guard against assuming coverage).
An alternation-group matcher that includes /api covers the route. Must be clean (the false positive that prefix-only matching used to raise).
Express LLM route; vulnerable has no limiter, safe applies a route-level express-rate-limit middleware.
Express LLM route protected by a global app.use(rateLimit(...)). Must be clean.
Express limiter configured but never applied to the app or route. Must flag.
CommonJS Express (require) LLM route; vulnerable has no limiter, safe applies a route-level express-rate-limit middleware.
Express route whose handler is a controller method in another file (the bcrypt sink lives there). Tests member-access handler resolution across files.

NextAuth/Auth.js Credentials provider: the authorize callback verifies a password (bcrypt), so the credential sign-in is the login endpoint. Vulnerable has no rate limiting (brute-force / CWE-307, must flag at the authorize). Safe enforces a custom per-identifier login limiter first (must be clean). Pattern confirmed on a real production app.

Next.js server action (`"use server"`) calling an LLM. Vulnerable has no rate limiting (must flag the action; a non-sensitive cookie/theme action in the same file must stay clean). Safe guards it with a custom project rate-limit helper (checkUserRateLimit), which must be recognized so a correctly-limited action is not a false positive (the real-world pattern from a scanned app).

An inline server action (its own `"use server"` directive inside a component, in a file with NO top-level directive) reaching the LLM sink with no rate limit. Requires modeling inline `"use server"` closures, not just top-level-directive files. Safe gates the same action with @upstash/ratelimit inside it.

Real-repo pattern (found on a private repo): a router created via a named import (`import { Router } from 'express'`, not express.Router()) in another file, with an AI route, mounted in app.ts. Safe applies an app-wide limiter that covers the mounted router (must be clean; guards a classification bug that flagged covered routers). Vulnerable has no limiter anywhere (must flag the named-import router route).

Precision case from a real repo: a route applies a limiter imported from a local wrapper module that re-exports an express-rate-limit instance (safe, must be clean). The vulnerable counterpart applies a local middleware that is NOT a limiter (a logger), so the route must still be flagged (guards against blanket-suppressing on any local import).

Cross-file mount resolution (D041), the scouthook pattern: a path-scoped app limiter `app.use('/api', rateLimit())` covers a router mounted under /api (safe, must be clean), but a sensitive router mounted at /auth is not covered (vulnerable, must flag). Guards the ~25-false-positive class found on a real repo.

Real-world CommonJS Express layout: an app mounts a router (require) defined in another file, whose handler is an exports.x controller method in a third file (bcrypt). Vulnerable has no limiter (flag in the routes file); safe applies an app-wide limiter that covers every mounted router (must be clean, the cross-file precision case).
False-positive control: a non-sensitive GET with no catalogued sink and no limiter. Must NOT be flagged.
Route wrapped in a custom guard the catalogue does not recognize. Per D024 (unknownGuard defaults to flag) this is flagged with an informative message naming the guard.
